title: How to think about your financial life as a system
subtitle: Isolated decisions compound into a life. Understanding the system is how you stop making the same mistakes twice.

body.content: Most people think about money one decision at a time: should I buy this, should I save that, is this the right time to invest. This is the wrong unit of analysis. Your financial life is a system — a set of interconnected flows, stocks, and decisions that compound over time. Understanding the system changes what you optimize for.
The three layers of a financial system

items: Flow layer: income in, expenses out. The gap between them is your operating surplus. This is the fuel for everything else.
Stock layer: what you own minus what you owe. Assets minus liabilities. This is your net worth — the accumulated result of past flow decisions.
Growth layer: how your stocks generate future flows. Investments that produce returns. Debt that has a cost. This layer is where compounding happens.

body.label: Systems thinking applied
body.content: A single decision affects all three layers. Taking on a mortgage increases your stock of assets and your stock of liabilities simultaneously. It converts a flow (rent) into a different flow (mortgage + maintenance). It creates a growth asset if the property appreciates, and a cost if rates rise. Evaluating it on any single dimension misses the full picture.

The most common financial error is optimizing one layer while ignoring the others. Cutting expenses aggressively while leaving capital idle. Growing net worth through property while carrying high-interest credit card debt. Investing in markets while having no emergency fund. Each of these is a system imbalance — and systems with imbalances eventually correct, often painfully.

Your financial life is not a series of separate decisions. It is a system. When you make a decision, ask how it affects the flow, the stock, and the growth layer. That is financial thinking — not financial anxiety.
